title: Severe Anorexia Nervosa (Restricting Type) in a 16-Year-Old Girl Admitted for Medical Stabilization at 71% of Expected Body Weight with Bradycardia (HR 38 Asleep), Hypotension, Hypothermia, Orthostasis, and QTc Prolongation: Preventing and Managing REFEEDING SYNDROME (Hypophosphatemia + Hypokalemia + Hypomagnesemia + Thiamine Depletion) with Cautious 'Start-Low-Go-Slow' Nutritional Rehabilitation, Electrolyte Repletion, Continuous Cardiac Monitoring, Structured Supervised Meals, and Family-Based Multidisciplinary Treatment
subtitle: ANOREXIA NERVOSA (AN) = a psychiatric eating disorder (DSM-5) with the HIGHEST mortality of any psychiatric illness (from medical complications + suicide) defined by: (1) restriction of energy intake relative to requirements leading to SIGNIFICANTLY LOW BODY WEIGHT for age/sex/development/health; (2) intense FEAR of gaining weight or persistent behavior interfering with weight gain despite low weight; (3) DISTURBANCE in the way body weight/shape is experienced, undue influence of weight on self-evaluation, or denial of the seriousness of low weight; SUBTYPES = RESTRICTING (weight loss via dieting/fasting/excessive exercise) vs BINGE-EATING/PURGING; severity by BMI in adults; in adolescents use % of expected body weight + growth curves (this patient 71% of expected body weight = severe). AN is NOT a lifestyle choice or vanity — it is a serious mental illness, frequently comorbid with anxiety, depression, OCD, and trauma, with high suicide risk. MEDICAL COMPLICATIONS OF STARVATION (multisystem; drive medical admission): CARDIAC — sinus BRADYCARDIA (adaptive hypometabolism; HR may be <40, especially asleep), HYPOTENSION, ORTHOSTASIS, reduced cardiac mass, pericardial effusion, prolonged QTc + arrhythmia risk, mitral valve prolapse; sudden cardiac death; HYPOTHERMIA; ELECTROLYTE abnormalities (worse with purging — hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis); ENDOCRINE — amenorrhea, low estrogen/testosterone, euthyroid-sick, hypoglycemia, growth/pubertal delay; BONE — osteopenia/osteoporosis; HEME — leukopenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia; GI — delayed gastric emptying, constipation, elevated transaminases; DERM — lanugo (fine body hair), dry skin, hair loss, carotenoderma, Russell's sign (knuckle calluses from self-induced vomiting) + dental erosion/parotid swelling if purging; RENAL; NEURO — pseudoatrophy. CRITERIA FOR MEDICAL HOSPITALIZATION (AAP/SAHM) include: HR <50 daytime / <45 at night (bradycardia), systolic BP <90, orthostatic changes (HR rise >20 or BP drop >10-20), hypothermia <36°C, <75% expected body weight (or acute rapid weight loss), electrolyte disturbance, ECG abnormality (prolonged QTc/arrhythmia), syncope, acute food refusal, uncontrolled bingeing/purging, hematemesis, or comorbid conditions requiring stabilization. THE CENTRAL INPATIENT DANGER = REFEEDING SYNDROME: when nutrition is reintroduced after prolonged starvation, the carbohydrate load triggers an INSULIN SURGE that drives PHOSPHATE, POTASSIUM, and MAGNESIUM intracellularly (and increases cellular uptake for anabolism), causing potentially fatal HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA (the HALLMARK — phosphate is needed for ATP + 2,3-DPG + cardiac/respiratory muscle), HYPOKALEMIA, and HYPOMAGNESEMIA; concurrent THIAMINE (B1) depletion (consumed in carbohydrate metabolism → Wernicke risk); and a shift to sodium/water RETENTION causing fluid overload + edema → manifesting as cardiac ARRHYTHMIA + heart failure, respiratory failure, rhabdomyolysis, seizures, delirium, and death, typically within the first days of refeeding. PREVENTION/MANAGEMENT OF REFEEDING SYNDROME: (1) identify high-risk patients (very low weight, minimal intake, prior low electrolytes, alcohol/purging); (2) CHECK baseline phosphate, potassium, magnesium, and glucose and CORRECT deficiencies BEFORE/while starting feeds; (3) give THIAMINE BEFORE or with the start of refeeding (and before any IV dextrose) to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy; (4) 'START LOW, GO SLOW' — begin at a conservative calorie level and advance gradually per protocol while monitoring (note: contemporary protocols favor somewhat higher starting calories with vigilant electrolyte monitoring + repletion rather than excessive caution that prolongs malnutrition — but always with close monitoring); (5) MONITOR electrolytes frequently (e.g., q12h initially) and REPLETE phosphate/potassium/magnesium proactively; (6) CONTINUOUS CARDIAC MONITORING + daily weights + strict intake/output + watch for edema/fluid overload; (7) prefer ORAL/enteral nutrition; avoid rapid IV dextrose/fluids. NURSING CARE (Basic Care & Comfort heavy): structured, SUPERVISED MEALS with a set meal plan + a calm, non-negotiating, supportive presence; OBSERVE during and for ~1 hour AFTER meals to prevent purging/hiding/discarding food + excessive exercise; standardized WEIGHTS (often 'blind'/back-to-scale, gowned, after voiding, same time/scale each morning) per protocol to reduce anxiety + ensure accuracy; monitor vitals/orthostatics/temperature + cardiac rhythm; provide warmth + comfort for hypothermia; manage constipation/early satiety/bloating; therapeutic, non-judgmental relationship that separates the person from the illness; assess SUICIDE risk; do NOT collude with the eating disorder (e.g., excessive low-cal substitutions) but also avoid power struggles. TREATMENT FRAMEWORK = medical stabilization → nutritional rehabilitation/weight restoration → PSYCHOTHERAPY: FAMILY-BASED TREATMENT (FBT/Maudsley) is the first-line, evidence-based treatment for adolescents (empowers PARENTS to take charge of refeeding/meals initially, then gradually returns control to the adolescent); CBT-E for adults; medications have a LIMITED role (no FDA-approved drug for AN; olanzapine may modestly help weight/obsessionality; SSRIs treat comorbid anxiety/depression but are ineffective for core AN at low weight); treat comorbidities; long-term outpatient multidisciplinary follow-up. DEVELOPMENTAL/ETHICAL: adolescent care is family-centered with parental involvement + the patient's assent; severe AN can impair insight/judgment, and life-threatening refusal may rarely require involuntary treatment, but the standard approach is engagement + FBT + the least restrictive effective care
scenario: ANOREXIA NERVOSA (AN) = a psychiatric eating disorder (DSM-5) with the HIGHEST mortality of any psychiatric illness (from medical complications + suicide) defined by: (1) restriction of energy intake relative to requirements leading to SIGNIFICANTLY LOW BODY WEIGHT for age/sex/development/health; (2) intense FEAR of gaining weight or persistent behavior interfering with weight gain despite low weight; (3) DISTURBANCE in the way body weight/shape is experienced, undue influence of weight on self-evaluation, or denial of the seriousness of low weight; SUBTYPES = RESTRICTING (weight loss via dieting/fasting/excessive exercise) vs BINGE-EATING/PURGING; severity by BMI in adults; in adolescents use % of expected body weight + growth curves (this patient 71% of expected body weight = severe). steps.q: Which of the following findings is MOST important for the nurse to recognize in this client?

opts.text: Begin aggressive high-calorie refeeding immediately with an IV dextrose infusion and large fluid boluses to reverse the malnutrition as fast as possible
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. Aggressive refeeding + IV dextrose/fluids precipitates REFEEDING SYNDROME (hypophosphatemia, arrhythmia, fluid overload, death) and Wernicke's. Use cautious advancement, oral/enteral preference, thiamine first, and electrolyte monitoring/repletion.

opts.text: Severe malnutrition at 71% of expected body weight (BMI 14.2) with anorexia nervosa, restricting type — a serious psychiatric illness with the highest mortality of any psychiatric disorder, not a lifestyle choice
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — severity of malnutrition + the seriousness of the illness frame the medical risk. RN role: recognize medical instability + the psychiatric illness.

opts.text: The sinus bradycardia (HR 38 asleep) is an immediate indication for atropine and a transcutaneous pacer regardless of rhythm or symptoms
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Sinus bradycardia of starvation in an asymptomatic, perfusing patient (sinus rhythm, no pauses/block) is an expected adaptation that improves with nutritional rehabilitation — not an indication for atropine/pacing. Monitor; treat symptomatic pathologic arrhythmia, not adaptive sinus bradycardia.

steps.q: Which statement BEST explains the significance of this client’s clinical findings?

opts.text: Because the patient says she just wants to be 'healthy' and looks calm, her denial of the seriousness of her low weight means she has good insight and meal supervision is unnecessary
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Denial/minimization of the seriousness of low weight is a feature of AN, not evidence of insight; supervised meals + monitoring remain essential. The illness distorts her perception of risk.

opts.text: Anorexia nervosa = restriction → significantly low body weight + intense fear of weight gain + body-image disturbance/denial of seriousness; a serious psychiatric illness (highest psychiatric mortality) with multisystem starvation complications
opts.rat: CORRECT — AN definition + seriousness. Foundation for recognizing medical + psychiatric risk.

opts.text: Serum electrolytes that look near-normal on admission mean the patient is not at risk for refeeding syndrome and feeds can be advanced rapidly
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Total-body phosphate/potassium/magnesium are depleted even when serum levels look near-normal; they fall sharply once feeding starts. Risk is based on weight/intake history, and cautious advancement + monitoring are still required.

steps.q: Which is the MOST likely explanation for this client’s presentation?

opts.text: NUTRITIONAL REHABILITATION via structured supervised meals r/t severe malnutrition — set meal plan, calm supervised meals, post-meal observation, activity restriction, accurate intake documentation
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Re-nourishment (done safely) is the definitive treatment + reverses many complications. RN role: supervised meals + behavioral monitoring.

opts.text: PSYCHIATRIC SAFETY r/t high suicide risk + comorbid anxiety/OCD — ongoing suicide-risk assessment + safe environment + psychiatry + therapeutic relationship
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Psychiatric safety is continuous + AN has high suicide risk. RN role: risk assessment + safe environment + alliance.

opts.text: CARDIAC stability + refeeding-syndrome prevention r/t bradycardia + QTc prolongation + electrolyte shifts — continuous telemetry, baseline + frequent phosphate/potassium/magnesium, thiamine before feeds, proactive repletion, cautious calorie advancement, watch for arrhythmia/edema
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The acute life threats (arrhythmia + refeeding syndrome) are cardiac/electrolyte. RN role: monitoring + repletion + cautious refeeding.

opts.text: ACCURATE MONITORING r/t weights/orthostatics/temperature/I&O — standardized blind weights, detect water-loading, orthostatic vitals, warmth for hypothermia, fluid-balance + edema surveillance
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Accurate data guides safe advancement + detects deterioration. RN role: standardized monitoring + comfort.

steps.q:
Negotiate meal content with the patient and substitute low-calorie 'safe' foods whenever she is anxious, to keep the peace

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — colludes with the eating disorder and undermines re-nourishment. Meal content is non-negotiable; provide support for the anxiety without changing the plan.

steps.q:
Allow unsupervised meals and bathroom use and let her exercise freely, since she is calm and cooperative

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — removes the supervision/observation/activity restriction needed to prevent purging/hiding food/compulsive exercise and to reduce cardiac demand. Calm cooperation does not eliminate eating-disorder behaviors.

steps.q:
Check baseline + q12h phosphate/potassium/magnesium, give thiamine before feeds, start nutrition at a conservative level, advance gradually, and replete electrolytes proactively (hold advancement if phosphate falls)

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.rat: Core refeeding-syndrome prevention. RN role: monitoring + thiamine + repletion + cautious advancement.

steps.q: Which action should the nurse take FIRST?

opts.text: Establish continuous cardiac telemetry + obtain orthostatic vitals/temperature + draw baseline phosphate/potassium/magnesium/glucose + ECG; apply warming measures + activity restriction
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Assess + monitor the acute cardiac/electrolyte risks and stabilize the environment. RN role: monitoring + baseline labs + safety.

opts.text: Provide structured supervised meals with support + observe during and after meals + initiate standardized blind weights + strict I&O + watch for edema/arrhythmia
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Deliver and monitor safe re-nourishment + detect refeeding complications. RN role: supervised meals + monitoring.

opts.text: Give thiamine before feeds + correct baseline electrolyte deficiencies, then begin nutrition at a conservative calorie level per refeeding protocol
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Prevent refeeding syndrome/Wernicke before/at the start of cautious feeding. RN role: thiamine + repletion + cautious initiation.

opts.text: Assess suicide risk + mental status, engage psychiatry, and build a therapeutic, non-judgmental relationship that externalizes the illness
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Psychiatric safety + alliance underpin treatment. RN role: risk assessment + therapeutic relationship + psychiatry.

steps.q:
Day 3 alternative — the patient becomes tearful and discloses passive suicidal thoughts and hopelessness; the nurse performs a focused suicide-risk assessment, ensures a safe environment, and notifies psychiatry urgently

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.rat: ⚠️ Emergence of suicidal ideation (AN carries high suicide risk). Appropriate response (assess + safe environment + psychiatry) is correct; the clinical status has worsened and requires escalation.

steps.q:
Admission-day telemetry shows HR 38 asleep in sinus rhythm, asymptomatic and perfusing, unchanged from the expected bradycardia of starvation on serial checks before nutrition has taken effect

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.rat: Asymptomatic sinus bradycardia of starvation is expected and will not change until nutritional rehabilitation progresses; serial checks before feeding effect show it unchanged (monitored, not an emergency in itself).

steps.q:
Day 10: HR ≥50 daytime sustained, normothermic, no orthostasis, stable electrolytes on oral repletion, QTc 448, steady weight gain, no refeeding complications; transitioning to specialized program with FBT

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.rat: Medical stabilization achieved with safe step-down planning. Excellent trajectory.
